title: Module Check: Linux Persistence and Privilege Escalation

questions.question: How does the SUID bit allow a low-privilege user to execute code as root?
questions.answer: The SUID bit on an executable sets the effective UID to the file owner (e.g., root) when executed, regardless of who runs it. The process inherits root privileges for its duration. If the binary allows code execution (e.g., a shell, vim, find with -exec), the attacker gains a root shell.

questions.question: Write the `find` command that lists all SUID-bit files on a Linux system.
questions.answer: `find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null` — searches from root, matches files with the SUID permission bit set (4000 in octal), suppresses permission errors. Adding `-ls` gives ownership details.

questions.question: Name four locations an attacker uses to establish persistence on Linux.
questions.answer: (1) ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile — runs on user login. (2) /etc/cron.d/ or crontab — scheduled execution. (3) systemd unit file in /etc/systemd/system/ — survives reboot. (4) LD_PRELOAD in /etc/ld.so.preload — injects a shared library into every process. Also: SSH authorized_keys, /etc/rc.local.

questions.question: What detection benefit does the Linux Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) provide for threat hunting?

options.A: FHS enforces file permission standards that block malware writes
options.B: FHS defines expected locations — executables outside standard paths are anomalous and easier to detect
options.C: FHS checksums system binaries at boot
options.D: FHS prevents modification of /etc without root

questions.explanation: FHS defines conventions: binaries in /usr/bin, libraries in /usr/lib, config in /etc. An executable in /tmp, /var/www, or a home directory is an immediate anomaly. Threat hunters use this baseline to filter noise from true positives. FHS has no enforcement mechanism — it is a convention, not a control.

questions.question: What is the difference between privilege escalation and lateral movement?

options.A: Privilege escalation moves between hosts; lateral movement elevates within one host
options.B: Privilege escalation elevates access level on the current host; lateral movement moves to a different host
options.C: Both terms describe the same technique at different stages of the kill chain
options.D: Lateral movement is only possible after full privilege escalation

questions.explanation: Privilege escalation: low-priv user → root on the same machine. Lateral movement: compromised host → another host in the network. Both are post-exploitation techniques but target different attack dimensions. Lateral movement does not require root; credential theft often enables lateral movement at user level.
